raw_case:
MRI diagnosis of a juvenile leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter: A rare case report from Morocco and literature review
Leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter is a new autosomal recessive inherited disorder disease entity. It is a chronic disease with a progressive course and episodes of exacerbation, the prevalence of which is still unknown. This disease usually occurs in children and is rare, particularly in the non-Caucasian population. Its main symptoms are ataxia and spasticity and MRI is a key diagnostic tool. We present a juvenile-onset Moroccan case of leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter. MRI findings in this case included linear high signal intensity on T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery images in the pons, in addition to typical abnormalities in the cerebral white matter. The diagnosis of leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter was based on clinical criteria and the typical appearance on a cerebral MRI.

Introduction

Leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter, also known as childhood ataxia with central hypomyelination and myelinopathia centralis diffusa, stands out as a prevalent inherited childhood disorder affecting white matter [1]. Initially identified in its infantile form in Canada in 1988, subsequent observations revealed its impact across all age groups. Although leukoencephalopathy with vanishing white matter is common among Caucasian individuals, cases have been documented in non-Caucasian individuals from South America and Asia, with an estimated incidence of 1 in 80,000 live births. In cases of existent parental consanguinity, the incidence reached 42 cases in 210 families [2].Manifesting as chronic and progressive childhood ataxia, spasticity and optic atrophy, vanishing white matter disease (VWM) was identified with the advent of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in 1993. Its nomenclature was established in 1997, and diagnostic criteria were defined, relying on clinical and MRI findings due to the absence of specific laboratory tests [3].The genetic basis involves mutations in any of the 5 genes encoding the eukaryotic translation initiation factor eIF2B. Despite residing in housekeeping genes, VWM predominantly affects the brain, particularly oligodendrocytes and astrocytes, though the exact vulnerability mechanisms remain unclear [4].This paper presents a case involving a 13-year-old boy without prior medical history who presented to our hospital with a 3-year history of progressive ataxia and postural instability. The patient's brain MRI strongly suggests VWM.

Case report

We report the case of a 13-year-old boy from Oujda, in the eastern part of Morocco. He is the first child of consanguineous parents, born after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery with a normal neonatal period, and his vaccination report was up-to-date. According to his parents, there was no developmental deterioration or regression during his childhood. His parents did not report a history of neurological and familial disorders.The patient was brought to our hospital with a history of progressive ataxia for 3 years, recurrent falls and postural instability. The patient presented a progressive disability in walking, dragging his feet around to move.On physical examination, his vital signs and anthropometric measurements were within the normal limit (BP: 94/40 mmHg, HR: 120/min, RR: 23/min, T°: 37.2°C, SpO2: 95%, blood sugar: 91 mg/dl) with no dysmorphic features.The neurological examination reveals a conscious patient with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15/15. The patient's pupils are equal and reactive. He can stand with a wide base of support, and his gait is spastic and unsteady with a decomposed half-turn. Hypermetria and generalised hypotonia are observed during movement. The patient's muscle strength is globally reduced, rated at 3 out of 5, with predominant muscular spasticity in the lower limbs. No sensory deficit is noted in the clinical examination. Osteotendinous reflex examination reveals brisk, widespread and polysynaptic reflexes. Cranial nerve examination is normal. The rest of the clinical examination shows no other abnormalities. The laboratory findings were normal.Brain and medullar MRIs were obtained and reported by our team of radiologists. Imaging findings showed diffuse and symmetrical supra- (Fig. 1) and infra-tentorial (Fig. 2) white matter high signal abnormalities in T2 and FLAIR images without restriction of diffusion or contrast enhancement. These findings were associated to some cystic changes in supratentorial peri-ventricular white matter for this child, especially in the centrum semiovale (Fig. 3). In addition, diffuse atrophy of the corpus callosum (Fig. 4) and spinal cord (Fig. 5) was found, all of which are considered important MRI features of VWM.Based on the patient's medical history, clinical examination and MRI fidings, the first diagnosis to emerge was VWM.Currently, there is no targeted treatment available for VWM. Instead, parents received counseling regarding the disease's causes, progression, and outcomes. They were advised to minimize stressful situations, prevent head trauma, and administer antipyretics and vaccinations. Regular follow-ups were recommended to monitor the condition's progression.

Discussion

VWM, also known as childhood ataxia with central hypomyelination (CACH), stands out as a prominent inherited childhood disorder affecting the white matter [1].Initially identified as a condition affecting young children, recent years have revealed a broad phenotypic spectrum with documented cases spanning various age groups [5].The disease's historical narrative dates to 1962, with Eicke's account of neuropathological findings in a 36-year-old woman who experienced gait difficulties and secondary amenorrhea at age 31. Subsequent reports by Watanabe, Muller, Girard, Anzil, Gessaga, Deisenhammer, Jellinger, Gautier and Graveleau further detailed similar cases [6]. Rediscovered in 1993 and 1994 by Hanefeld and Schiffmann, the disease was characterised as a childhood-onset, progressive leukoencephalopathy with autosomal recessive inheritance [7].The classical form of VWM disease typically presents between the ages of 2 and 6, showing signs of chronic progressive neurological decline characterized by cerebellar ataxia, mild spasticity, and relatively minor cognitive impairment. Additionally, individuals with this form of the disease may experience optic atrophy and seizures as accompanying symptoms [8].However, diverse variants have surfaced, including adolescent and adult onset [9]. Some adults exhibit initial signs through seizures, and others present with psychiatric symptoms, dementia or motor decline. Onset variations influence the disease's pace, with later onset cases generally having a more protracted course, although unexpected rapid progression leading to death within months is possible. Early infantile or antenatal onset may result in severe mortality before the age of 2 [10].A distinctive aspect of VWM is its episodic nature, marked by rapid neurological deterioration triggered by stresses such as fever and minor head trauma, adding complexity to its clinical presentation [11]. The disease can be diagnosed through MRI, which typically reveals widespread and symmetrical involvement of the cerebral white matter. U fibres, internal capsules and the outer part of the corpus callosum are spared, but cortical grey matter remains unaffected. As time progresses, a gradual rarefaction and cystic degeneration of the white matter occurs, and it is ultimately replaced by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-like signal intensity on T1WI, T2WI and FLAIR images. Radiating stripes on T1WI and FLAIR images suggest the presence of remaining normal white matter tracts. In later stages, cerebellar atrophy may occur, ranging from mild to severe and primarily affecting the vermis. Unlike other conditions, there is no contrast enhancement in VWM, and diffusion-weighted imaging reveals increased diffusion in areas of rarefaction and cystic degeneration [12,13].Van der Knaap et al. have described obligatory and suggestive MRI criteria for VWM diagnosis [6]:•Obligatory criteria:-Cerebral white matter shows either diffuse or extensive signal abnormalities; the immediately subcortical white matter may be spared.-Part or all of the abnormal white matter has a signal intensity close to or the same as cerebrospinal fluid on proton density or FLAIR images, suggestive of white matter rarefaction or cystic destruction.-If proton density and FLAIR images suggest that all cerebral white matter has disappeared, there is a fluid-filled distance between ependymal lining and the cortex, but not a total collapse of the white matter-The disappearance of the cerebral white matter occurs in a diffuse ‘melting away’ pattern.-The temporal lobes are relatively spared, in the extent of the abnormal signal, degree of cystic destruction or both.-Cerebellar white matter may be abnormal, but does not contain cysts.-There is no contrast enhancement.•Suggestive criteria:-Radiating stripes are observed within the abnormal white matter on sagittal and coronal T1-weighted or FLAIR images, with dots and stripes visible as cross-sections on axial images.-Lesions are present within the central tegmental tracts located in the pontine tegmentum.-Involvement of the corpus callosum is restricted to the inner rim, while the outer rim remains unaffected.Examination of bodily fluids has uncovered only a small number of biochemical indicators for VWM. A consistent increase in cerebrospinal fluid glycine levels and an elevated ratio of cerebrospinal fluid to plasma glycine concentrations were identified as the first markers. A more recent biomarker for VWM is the reduced concentration of asialotransferrin in cerebrospinal fluid. However, the practical utility of these biomarkers is constrained because of the necessity for lumbar puncture and the uncertain sensitivity and specificity of each marker [14].VWM lacks a targeted treatment. Essential measures involve avoiding stressors that can trigger deterioration in VWM patients. Basic yet crucial steps include widespread use of antibiotics and antipyretics, receiving vaccinations and refraining from contact sports. Despite their importance, these measures alone are insufficient to halt the onset or progression of the disease [15].

Conclusion

VWM poses a significant challenge due to the absence of a specific treatment. Although mitigating stressors, incorporating antibiotics, antipyretics, vaccinations and avoiding contact sports are vital measures, they fall short of preventing the disease's onset or progression. VWM's prognosis remains uncertain, underscoring the urgent need for continued research and advancements in understanding this complex disorder. Enhancing the health care providers knowledge will enable us to develop more effective interventions, ultimately providing hope for improved outcomes and quality of life for individuals affected by VWM.

Patient consent

Written informed consent for publication of this case study was obtained from the patient's family.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** A 13-year-old Moroccan boy from consanguineous parents with no prior medical history.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressive ataxia for 3 years, recurrent falls, and postural instability.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive difficulty in walking and foot dragging over 3 years.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** First child of consanguineous parents, normal neonatal period, no developmental delays or regressions, vaccinations up-to-date.
- **Family History:** No family history of neurological disorders or other significant conditions.
- **Physical Examination:** Normal vital signs; gait is spastic and unsteady; generalized hypotonia; muscle weakness (3/5 strength); spasticity in lower limbs; brisk and polysynaptic deep tendon reflexes; normal cranial nerve function and no sensory deficits. No dysmorphic features or other systemic abnormalities.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Normal laboratory findings; MRI findings include:
  - Diffuse, symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in supratentorial and infratentorial white matter.
  - Cystic degenerations in periventricular white matter.
  - Corpus callosum and spinal cord atrophy.
  - Lack of diffusion restriction or contrast enhancement.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The primary diagnosis consideration was **Leukoencephalopathy with Vanishing White Matter (VWM)**, based on the clinical presentation of progressive ataxia, spasticity, and MRI findings consistent with typical patterns of VWM.
  
- **Rationale for Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
  - Other leukodystrophies (e.g., metachromatic leukodystrophy, adrenoleukodystrophy): These conditions were ruled out because they present distinct MRI patterns or biochemical markers not identified in this patient.
  - Multiple sclerosis: Excluded due to lack of acute or relapsing episodes, contrast enhancement on MRI, or other inflammatory markers.
  - Brain infections or autoimmune conditions: No evidence of systemic or inflammatory lab markers, clinical signs of infection, or enhancing lesions.
  - Vascular or structural etiologies: Absence of ischemic or vascular lesions on MRI, and no history of significant trauma.

- **Rationale for Confirming VWM Diagnosis:**
  - Clinical presentation: Symptoms of persistent and progressive neurological decline characterized by ataxia and spasticity are typical for VWM.
  - MRI findings: Diffuse symmetric supratentorial and infratentorial white matter abnormalities, cystic degeneration, corpus callosum and spinal cord atrophy, and absence of contrast enhancement meet the diagnostic MRI criteria for VWM.
  - Genetic predisposition: Consanguinity among parents increases likelihood of autosomal recessive disorders like VWM.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Leukoencephalopathy with Vanishing White Matter (VWM), confirmed by clinical data and typical MRI findings. The absence of laboratory diagnostic markers was addressed with reliance on characteristic imaging features and clinical progression.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to figure this out. The patient is a 13-year-old boy from consanguineous parents, which immediately makes me think about autosomal recessive disorders. His main issues are progressive ataxia and spasticity over three years. Let's start by breaking down the symptoms and test results.

First, the progressive ataxia and spasticity in the lower limbs. The MRI shows white matter abnormalities, cystic degeneration, and atrophy of the corpus callosum and spinal cord. The white matter changes are diffuse and symmetrical, which is typical of leukodystrophies. Leukodystrophies are genetic disorders affecting the myelin. Since the parents are consanguineous, an autosomal recessive condition is likely.

Looking at the MRI findings: T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in white matter, cystic degeneration, and corpus callosum atrophy. The absence of contrast enhancement or diffusion restriction suggests it's not an acute inflammatory process like ADEM or a stroke. The cystic lesions in periventricular areas make me think of conditions like Megalencephalic Leukoencephalopathy with subcortical cysts (MLC), but the patient doesn't have megalencephaly mentioned here. Another possibility is Vanishing White Matter Disease (VWM), but that usually has a more episodic deterioration.

Wait, the spinal cord atrophy is also noted. That could point towards conditions like Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP), but HSP typically has more spasticity and less prominent white matter changes. However, some complex forms of HSP can have brain involvement. Alternatively, the combination of ataxia and spasticity with white matter abnormalities might suggest a mitochondrial disorder, but the MRI pattern here seems more like a leukodystrophy.

Another important point: the normal lab results. Many metabolic disorders present with specific biochemical abnormalities, but here they're normal. So maybe a leukodystrophy that doesn't show up on routine blood tests. The cystic degeneration in periventricular white matter is a key clue. Conditions like Alexander disease can have cysts but usually have other features like brainstem involvement. Canavan disease has spongiform degeneration, but the onset is earlier and there's developmental delay. This patient had normal development, so maybe a later-onset leukodystrophy.

The corpus callosum atrophy and spinal cord atrophy make me think of disorders that affect both the brain and spinal cord. Maybe something like X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD), but that typically has contrast enhancement in the active stages and the spinal cord isn't usually involved. Also, ALD is X-linked, and the patient's parents are consanguineous, but he's male. However, the MRI here lacks contrast enhancement, so maybe not ALD.

Wait, another possibility: Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS). That presents with spastic ataxia, but the MRI findings usually include hyperintensity of the pyramidal tracts and cerebellar atrophy. The patient's MRI shows corpus callosum atrophy, not sure if that's typical for ARSACS. Also, the white matter changes here are more diffuse.

Looking back, the key MRI features are diffuse white matter hyperintensity, cystic degeneration, and atrophy. There's a leukodystrophy called Childhood Ataxia with Central Hypomyelination (CACH) or Vanishing White Matter (VWM) disease. VWM typically presents with progressive ataxia and spasticity, triggered by stress. MRI shows diffuse white matter abnormalities that become cystic and rarefied, which fits with the cystic degeneration here. The corpus callosum atrophy could be part of the progression. However, VWM often has a fluctuating course with episodes of deterioration, which isn't mentioned here. But maybe this case is a more slowly progressive form.

Another consideration is Pelizaeus-Merzbacher Disease (PMD), which is X-linked and involves hypomyelination. However, symptoms usually start in infancy with nystagmus and hypotonia, progressing to spasticity. The MRI in PMD shows a "tigroid" pattern. The patient's MRI doesn't mention that, but maybe the cystic changes aren't typical for PMD.

Alternatively, maybe a peroxisomal disorder, but the labs were normal. Zellweger syndrome is severe and presents early. Adrenoleukodystrophy as mentioned earlier. Maybe Aicardi-Goutières syndrome, but that usually has calcifications and CSF lymphocytosis, which aren't noted here.

Wait, the MRI shows no diffusion restriction, which rules out acute ischemic changes. The absence of contrast enhancement suggests it's not an active inflammatory process. So, likely a genetic leukodystrophy. The combination of spastic ataxia, white matter changes with cystic degeneration, and consanguinity points towards a leukodystrophy with autosomal recessive inheritance.

Looking up the MRI features again: periventricular cystic degeneration. This is a hallmark of Megalencephalic leukoencephalopathy with subcortical cysts (MLC). But MLC usually presents with macrocephaly and early-onset. The patient here doesn't have megalencephaly. However, there's a variant of MLC without macrocephaly. Hmm, but the age of onset here is 10 years (since he's 13 now with 3 years of symptoms). MLC typically starts earlier, but maybe a later-onset form.

Alternatively, maybe Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis (CTX), which can present with ataxia and spasticity. However, CTX has tendon xanthomas and typically elevated cholestanol levels, which weren't mentioned here. The MRI in CTX can show cerebellar and cerebral white matter lesions, but the cystic degeneration might not be typical.

Wait, the presence of spinal cord atrophy is a key point. Some leukodystrophies affect the spinal cord. For example, Krabbe disease can have spinal nerve root involvement, but MRI typically shows thalamic hyperdensities and demyelination. Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD) presents with confluent white matter changes and a "tigroid" appearance, but spinal cord involvement can occur. However, MLD usually has low arylsulfatase A activity, which would show up in labs. The labs here are normal, so maybe not MLD.

Another thought: The patient has corpus callosum atrophy. This can be seen in disorders like Alexander disease, but again, the other features don't fit. Alternatively, 4H syndrome (Hypomyelination, Hypodontia, Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism), but the patient doesn't have those other features.

Wait, the MRI shows spinal cord atrophy. Could this be a spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA) with white matter involvement? Most SCAs are autosomal dominant, but consanguinity suggests recessive. Autosomal recessive SCAs like SCAR8 (SYNE1-related) can have cerebellar atrophy and spasticity. But the MRI here shows white matter hyperintensity and cystic changes, which might not align with typical SCAs.

Alternatively, Ataxia-Telangiectasia (A-T) has ataxia and telangiectasias, but those aren't mentioned here. Also, A-T usually has elevated AFP, which wasn't noted.

Putting it all together: consanguinity, progressive ataxia and spasticity, white matter changes with cystic degeneration, corpus callosum and spinal cord atrophy. The combination of spastic ataxia with white matter abnormalities and cysts suggests a leukodystrophy. The presence of cysts in the periventricular area, along with lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction, makes me think of VWM (Vanishing White Matter) disease. VWM can present in childhood with progressive ataxia and spasticity, and MRI shows cystic degeneration of the white matter. The spinal cord atrophy might be a part of the progression. Additionally, VWM is autosomal recessive, fitting the consanguinity. The normal lab tests also align since VWM is due to mutations in EIF2B genes, which aren't detected by routine labs. The absence of megalencephaly makes MLC less likely. So, the most probable diagnosis is Vanishing White Matter Disease.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 13-year-old boy from consanguineous parents, raising suspicion for an autosomal recessive disorder. Progressive ataxia, spasticity, and MRI findings of diffuse white matter abnormalities with cystic degeneration point to a leukodystrophy.  
<step 2> The MRI features—symmetrical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, cystic periventricular white matter degeneration, and corpus callosum/spinal cord atrophy—are inconsistent with acute inflammation (no diffusion restriction/contrast enhancement) but align with chronic genetic leukodystrophies.  
<step 3> The absence of megalencephaly and developmental delays rules out Megalencephalic Leukoencephalopathy with subcortical cysts (MLC). Normal labs exclude metabolic disorders like Metachromatic Leukodystrophy (MLD) or mitochondrial diseases.  
<step 4> Vanishing White Matter Disease (VWM) is strongly considered due to the combination of progressive ataxia, spasticity, cystic white matter rarefaction, and autosomal recessive inheritance. VWM typically lacks systemic/metabolic abnormalities and is linked to mutations in *EIF2B* genes.  
<step 5> Corpus callosum atrophy and spinal cord involvement further support VWM, as advanced stages of the disease involve widespread white matter degeneration and atrophy.  

### Answer:
Vanishing White Matter Disease (VWM)
